hook: A shy accountant who loved to run once blurted out the name of a company that did not exist, then spent the next twenty years nearly going broke to make it real. This is the raw, unglamorous origin of Nike, and the strange hollowness its founder felt the night he finally won.

p: In nineteen sixty-two a twenty-four-year-old Phil Knight ran the foggy streets of Portland before dawn, chasing something he could not yet name. He called it the Crazy Idea. At Stanford he had written a business-school paper arguing that just as cheap, excellent Japanese cameras had toppled the dominant German brands, Japanese factories could do the same to the German running shoes, Adidas and Puma, that ruled the sport. He got a decent grade, and then, unlike almost anyone who writes a school paper, he decided to go and do it.

He talked his conservative father into funding a trip around the world, and the real business happened in Kobe, Japan. Knight, an unknown American kid with no company and no money, talked his way into a meeting with Onitsuka, maker of the Tiger shoe. When the executives asked which firm he represented, he panicked and invented one on the spot, naming it Blue Ribbon Sports after the ribbons he had won as a boy. They nodded, agreed to send samples, and a company that had existed for ten seconds took its first breath.

On the same trip he climbed to the Acropolis in Athens and stood before the temple of Athena Nike, goddess of victory. He did not know it yet, but that ancient word was quietly planting itself in his mind, waiting years to bloom.

When the sample Tigers arrived, Knight mailed two pairs to the one opinion that mattered most, his old Oregon track coach Bill Bowerman, a brilliant eccentric obsessed with shaving weight off a runner's shoe. Bowerman did not just approve. He wanted in as a partner, and in a very Bowerman move he insisted Knight hold the controlling fifty-one percent so his former runner would be the boss. Still tinkering years later, Bowerman poured liquid rubber into the family waffle iron, ruined it, and out of that reckless breakfast experiment came the waffle sole, a light, gripping tread no lab had produced.

Knight was too practical, or too scared, to quit his day job. He worked as an accountant and sold Tigers on nights and weekends out of the trunk of his green Plymouth Valiant, driving to track meets across the Northwest. He sold out constantly, because he was a runner selling to runners, and his sheer belief in the shoe did the pitching the shy man never could.

As orders grew he hired Jeff Johnson, his first full-time employee and a zealot who threw himself into the mission. Johnson kept obsessive index cards on each runner's size, injuries, and race times, and wrote them long heartfelt letters, inventing grassroots customer devotion before anyone had a word for it. He also flooded Knight with letters that Knight, being Knight, often left unanswered. Johnson was the first of the misfits, the oddballs and outcasts who would build this company precisely because they fit in nowhere else.

The engine of dread through the middle of this book is not a rival. It is cash flow. Knight lived by one rule, grow or die, so he poured every dollar back into a bigger order: sell a thousand pairs this season, order two thousand for next, then three thousand, betting the whole company on the next shipment arriving and selling in time. Sales soared every year while the bank balance hovered a breath above empty.

His bank, First National Bank of Oregon, could not understand a man who kept no cash reserves on purpose. His banker begged him to slow down and build equity, and Knight ignored him, because slowing down felt like dying. Finally the bank did the thing he feared most and cut off his credit, leaving him owing the Japanese for a huge shipment with no way to pay.

Salvation came from an unexpected corner. A giant Japanese trading house, Nissho Iwai, agreed to finance his shoe imports, stepping in exactly where the American bank had walked away. Nissho saw the growth and the promise where the stodgy bankers saw only risk, and it quietly became Blue Ribbon's lifeline. Remember that name, because it comes back to save Knight's life.
sections.pull: On paper it was a rocket ship. In the bank account it was always a hair away from zero.

p: Blue Ribbon's fatal weakness was that it sold only one thing, someone else's shoes. So when an Onitsuka executive named Kitami turned cold, shipped late, and began flirting with other American distributors, Knight was terrified. Then he glimpsed the truth inside an unattended folder: a secret list of the distributors Onitsuka planned to replace him with, after all the years he had spent building demand from the trunk of his car.

A lesser man would have begged. Knight decided instead to make his own shoes and launch his own brand, and he needed a name and a logo fast, because the factory was already waiting. The room hated every option, and Knight himself favored the frankly terrible Dimension Six. At the last possible moment Jeff Johnson offered a name that had come to him in a half-dream: Nike, the goddess of victory whose temple Knight had stood before in Athens. Nobody loved it, but time had run out, so they chose victory almost by accident.

For the logo he paid a Portland State design student, Carolyn Davidson, to sketch something that suggested motion. She produced a simple curved check, the Swoosh, and again the team picked it mostly because the deadline was upon them. Then Blue Ribbon fought Onitsuka in court, won its freedom and damages, and walked out an independent company with its own brand. The break that had looked like it would kill Knight instead set him free.
sections.pull: The Swoosh cost thirty-five dollars, and Knight said only, I don't love it, but it'll grow on me.

p: The soul of a company is never its balance sheet. The soul of early Nike was Steve Prefontaine, the fearless Oregon distance runner who raced from the front, all out, daring anyone to keep up. Pre wore the shoes and embodied the mission, until he died in a car crash at twenty-four with his greatest races still ahead of him. Knight writes about the loss as a wound that never closed.

The company ran on that kind of devotion. Bob Woodell, paralyzed in an accident, ran operations from a wheelchair with a steadiness that anchored the chaos, and at one desperate moment his working-class parents handed Knight their entire life savings to keep the shaky company alive. Penny, once Knight's accounting student and bookkeeper, became his wife and the anchor of his personal life. This was an enterprise financed and staffed by faith, not venture capital.

As Nike grew, Knight's lieutenants gathered at raucous retreats where they screamed, insulted each other, drank, and hashed out the future with no corporate politeness at all. They named themselves, with self-deprecating affection, the Buttfaces. Those meetings tell you everything about the culture Knight built: irreverent, fiercely loyal, allergic to pretension, and bonded by a shared sense of being underdogs taking on the giants.

Knight never stopped doubling down, and in nineteen seventy-five it nearly ended everything. Nike had grown so fast, on financing so stretched and unconventional, that the cash simply was not there to cover what it owed. The bank, alarmed by numbers that did not seem to add up, froze the company's accounts overnight, and suddenly Nike could not touch its own money.

Then it got worse. Suspecting fraud, the bank alerted the FBI, and a man who had done nothing but chase his Crazy Idea and reinvest every dollar as fast as he could now faced not just bankruptcy but a federal investigation, his reputation and possibly his freedom on the line.

And once again, in the darkest hour, salvation came from Japan. Nissho, the trading house that had been his lifeline for years, covered the gap, vouched for Nike, and pulled the company back from the abyss. Knight survived not through clever American finance but through the loyalty of a partner who had believed in him when his own country's banks would not.

In December of nineteen eighty, after nearly two decades spent one bad month from ruin, Nike went public. Overnight the value of Knight's stake was written down for all the world to see, and he was worth roughly one hundred and seventy-eight million dollars. The man who had sold Tigers from a car trunk was suddenly rich beyond anything he could have imagined.

And that night he felt strangely, profoundly empty. He and Penny went out to dinner and the moment fell flat, anticlimactic, hollow. All the fear and sacrifice and obsession, and the great payoff arrived and did not feel like anything at all. The money, it turned out, had never really been the point.

Looking back as an older man, Knight's deepest regrets are not about business but about time, the hours he did not give his family while he was lost in the company's endless problems, and about his son Matthew, who died young in a diving accident, a grief no fortune could touch. His parting counsel is plain: chase a calling and not just a paycheck, give the world the best of yourself and hold nothing back, and never forget that a business is a living thing made of people and their belief. He was living Just Do It long before Nike ever printed the words.
sections.pull: The chase had been the thing. The chase had been the meaning.

takeaways: Chase a calling, not just a paycheck. Name the pursuit that genuinely lights you up the way running lit up Knight, and start moving toward it before you feel ready.
Sell something you truly believe in, to people whose needs you share. Knight was a runner selling to runners, and his conviction did the persuading his shyness never could.
Do not let one supplier or partner control your fate. Knight was almost destroyed because he only resold someone else's shoes, so build your own product and leverage before anyone can cut you off.
If you bet everything on growth, line up your financing before the cash crunch, not during it. Cultivate the backer, as Knight cultivated Nissho, who will still be there when your bank walks away.
Build your team from loyal believers, not just polished resumes. Nike was carried through its worst years by misfits who cared more than they impressed.
Guard your time with the people you love. Knight won everything the world can offer and still regretted the years he did not give his family, a bill no fortune can repay.

When reasonable people would quit, survive one more crisis. What built Nike was not genius but the stubbornness to endure one more frozen account, one more near-bankruptcy, and get up the next morning anyway.
